steps.title: Understand the solution condition
steps.content:
The system has at least one solution when the two equations are not parallel. This happens when either:
  • The equations represent the same line (infinitely many solutions)
  • The equations have different slopes (one unique intersection point)
If they were parallel with different intercepts, there would be no solution—and that's what we must avoid.

steps.title: Check equation I: Is it a scalar multiple
steps.content:
Test whether $9x + 4.5y = 7.5$ is a multiple of the given equation $6x + 3y = 5$.

Compute the ratio for each component:
$$\frac{9}{6} = 1.5, \quad \frac{4.5}{3} = 1.5, \quad \frac{7.5}{5} = 1.5$$
Since all ratios equal $1.5$, equation I is exactly $1.5 \times (6x + 3y = 5)$. The two equations represent the same line, so the system has infinitely many solutions. Equation I ✓ produces at least one solution.

steps.title: Check equation II: Do the slopes differ
steps.content:
For the system to have no solution, the lines would need to be parallel (same slope, different intercepts). Test whether $9x - 4.5y = 7.5$ has the same slope as $6x + 3y = 5$.

Rewrite each in slope-intercept form:
$$6x + 3y = 5 \implies y = -2x + \frac{5}{3} \quad (\text{slope} = -2)$$
$$9x - 4.5y = 7.5 \implies y = 2x - \frac{5}{3} \quad (\text{slope} = 2)$$
Since $-2 \neq 2$, the lines have different slopes and intersect at exactly one point. Equation II ✓ produces at least one solution.

steps.title: Both equations work
steps.content: Both equation I and equation II result in a system with at least one solution. That matches choice C — C is correct.
